abstract: With the growing adoption of large language model agents in persistent real-world roles, they naturally encounter continuous streams of tasks. A key limitation, however, is their failure to learn from the accumulated interaction history, forcing them to discard valuable insights and repeat past errors. We propose ReasoningBank, a novel memory framework that distills generalizable reasoning strategies from an agent's self-judged successful and failed experiences. At test time, an agent retrieves relevant memories from ReasoningBank to inform its interaction and then integrates new learnings back, enabling it to become more capable over time. Building on this powerful experience learner, we further introduce memory-aware test-time scaling (MaTTS), which accelerates and diversifies this learning process by scaling up the agent's interaction experience. By allocating more compute to each task, the agent generates abundant, diverse experiences that provide rich contrastive signals for synthesizing higher-quality memory. The better memory in turn guides more effective scaling, establishing a powerful synergy between memory and test-time scaling. Across web browsing and software engineering benchmarks, ReasoningBank consistently outperforms existing memory mechanisms that store raw trajectories or only successful task routines, improving both effectiveness and efficiency; MaTTS further amplifies these gains. These findings establish memory-driven experience scaling as a new scaling dimension, enabling agents to self-evolve with emergent behaviors naturally arise. Our code can be found at this https URL.

keyInsights: Distilling generalizable reasoning strategies from an agent's self-judged successful *and* failed experiences significantly enhances performance, generalization, and efficiency in multi-turn interactive tasks.
A synergistic relationship exists between high-quality, reasoning-focused memory and test-time scaling, where memory guides scaled exploration, and scaled experiences, in turn, enrich memory quality.

The framework enables agents to achieve continuous self-evolution and develop emergent, more complex strategies (e.g., from procedural to compositional reasoning) by effectively internalizing lessons from past interactions.
originalProblem: Large Language Model (LLM) agents lack the ability to systematically learn from past interactions, leading to repetitive errors and a failure to build upon accumulated insights over time.

Existing agent memory systems primarily store raw interaction trajectories or successful routines, often failing to extract high-level, transferable reasoning patterns or derive lessons from failures.

The application of Test-Time Scaling (TTS) to multi-turn interactive agents, particularly in conjunction with memory mechanisms, has been underexplored, potentially leading to redundant exploration without optimized learning signals.
results: ReasoningBank consistently outperformed baseline memory methods, boosting success rates by +4.6 to +8.3 percentage points and reducing average interaction steps by up to 2.8 across WebArena, Mind2Web, and SWE-Bench-Verified benchmarks.

MaTTS consistently exceeded vanilla test-time scaling, showing that memory-aware coordination and aggregation are crucial for effective scaling, with parallel MaTTS achieving 55.1% success rate vs. 52.4% for vanilla TTS at scaling factor k=5.

A bidirectional synergy was observed: high-quality memory from ReasoningBank amplified the benefits of test-time scaling (e.g., 49.7% to 55.1% SR with memory vs. 39.0% to 42.2% SR without), and scaling, when paired with ReasoningBank, led to the curation of higher-quality memory (Pass@1 rose from 49.7% to 53.0%).
solution: Developed ReasoningBank, a memory framework that distills past experiences into structured, transferrable knowledge units called memory items, abstracting low-level execution details while preserving generalizable reasoning patterns.

Implemented a closed-loop process for ReasoningBank, involving memory retrieval to guide current tasks, differentiated memory extraction (for both successful and failed trajectories), and straightforward memory consolidation.

Introduced Memory-aware Test-Time Scaling (MaTTS), which leverages ReasoningBank to generate diverse explorations (via parallel and sequential scaling) to provide rich contrastive signals for synthesizing higher-quality memory.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Google Cloud AI Research and the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign developed ReasoningBank, a memory framework for LLM agents that distills generalizable reasoning strategies from self-judged successful and failed experiences, combined with Memory-aware Test-Time Scaling (MaTTS). This approach improved agent success rates by up to 8.3 percentage points and reduced interaction steps by up to 2.8 across web navigation and software engineering tasks, fostering continuous self-evolution.

title: ReasoningBank: Scaling Agent Self-Evolving with Reasoning Memory

abstract: While large language model (LLM) agents can effectively use external tools for complex real-world tasks, they require memory systems to leverage historical experiences. Current memory systems enable basic storage and retrieval but lack sophisticated memory organization, despite recent attempts to incorporate graph databases. Moreover, these systems' fixed operations and structures limit their adaptability across diverse tasks. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a novel agentic memory system for LLM agents that can dynamically organize memories in an agentic way. Following the basic principles of the Zettelkasten method, we designed our memory system to create interconnected knowledge networks through dynamic indexing and linking. When a new memory is added, we generate a comprehensive note containing multiple structured attributes, including contextual descriptions, keywords, and tags. The system then analyzes historical memories to identify relevant connections, establishing links where meaningful similarities exist. Additionally, this process enables memory evolution - as new memories are integrated, they can trigger updates to the contextual representations and attributes of existing historical memories, allowing the memory network to continuously refine its understanding. Our approach combines the structured organization principles of Zettelkasten with the flexibility of agent-driven decision making, allowing for more adaptive and context-aware memory management. Empirical experiments on six foundation models show superior improvement against existing SOTA baselines. The source code for evaluating performance is available at this https URL, while the source code of the agentic memory system is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: LLM-driven dynamic organization, linking, and evolution of memory (agentic memory) substantially enhance LLM agents' capabilities for complex, long-term reasoning.
Mimicking human knowledge organization methods like Zettelkasten, by creating interconnected and flexible knowledge networks, allows for emergent understanding and adaptability in AI agents.
An agentic approach to memory management can drastically reduce operational costs by enabling selective and context-aware retrieval, minimizing token usage in LLM interactions.
originalProblem: Existing memory systems for LLM agents lack sophisticated organization, relying on fixed, predefined schemas that hinder the formation of innovative connections.
Current memory systems offer limited adaptability and generalization, restricting agents' ability to evolve knowledge and perform effectively in diverse, long-term tasks.
LLM agents struggle with complex, multi-hop reasoning over extended conversational histories due to static or rigidly structured memory management.
results: A-Mem consistently outperformed baselines on the LoCoMo dataset, achieving up to a six-fold improvement in ROUGE-L for multi-hop tasks with non-GPT models and more than doubling GPT-4o-mini's performance in multi-hop reasoning.

It reduced token usage per memory operation by 85-93% compared to baselines, leading to estimated operational costs of less than $0.0003 per operation and demonstrating high scalability with minimal increases in retrieval time for up to 1 million memories.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical and complementary roles of both the Link Generation and Memory Evolution modules, showing substantial performance degradation when either or both were removed, highlighting their necessity for effective agentic memory.
solution: A-Mem proposes an agentic memory system, inspired by the Zettelkasten method, which dynamically organizes memories and allows for continuous knowledge evolution.
It constructs structured memory notes with LLM-generated keywords, tags, and rich contextual descriptions, creating atomic and self-contained knowledge units.

The system autonomously generates links between new and historical memories based on semantic similarity and dynamically updates the contexts and attributes of existing memories for continuous refinement.
paper_summary.summary: A-Mem introduces a dynamic, agentic memory system for LLM agents, inspired by the Zettelkasten method, enabling autonomous organization, evolution, and retrieval of knowledge for long-term interactions. It achieves up to a six-fold improvement in complex multi-hop reasoning tasks and reduces memory operation token usage by 85-93% compared to existing baselines.

title: A-MEM: Agentic Memory for LLM Agents

abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents face fundamental limitations in long-horizon reasoning due to finite context windows, making effective memory management critical. Existing methods typically handle long-term memory (LTM) and short-term memory (STM) as separate components, relying on heuristics or auxiliary controllers, which limits adaptability and end-to-end optimization. In this paper, we propose Agentic Memory (AgeMem), a unified framework that integrates LTM and STM management directly into the agent's policy. AgeMem exposes memory operations as tool-based actions, enabling the LLM agent to autonomously decide what and when to store, retrieve, update, summarize, or discard information. To train such unified behaviors, we propose a three-stage progressive reinforcement learning strategy and design a step-wise GRPO to address sparse and discontinuous rewards induced by memory operations. Experiments on five long-horizon benchmarks demonstrate that AgeMem consistently outperforms strong memory-augmented baselines across multiple LLM backbones, achieving improved task performance, higher-quality long-term memory, and more efficient context usage.

keyInsights: Unifying LTM and STM management within the agent's policy, rather than treating them as separate, external modules, significantly improves long-horizon reasoning.
Treating memory operations as explicit, learnable tools empowers the LLM agent to adapt its memory strategy based on task demands and learned experience.
A progressive RL strategy with long-range credit assignment effectively overcomes the challenges of sparse and discontinuous rewards inherent in optimizing memory-related actions.
originalProblem: LLM agents are fundamentally limited by finite context windows, hindering their ability to perform long-horizon reasoning tasks.
Existing memory management approaches treat long-term (LTM) and short-term (STM) memories as independent, loosely coupled modules, leading to fragmented and suboptimal performance.
Current methods for memory control often rely on handcrafted rules or auxiliary expert models, increasing inference costs and limiting adaptability.
results: AgeMem achieved the highest average performance on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (41.96%) and Qwen3-4B-Instruct (54.31%), outperforming memory-augmented baselines by 4.82 to 8.57 percentage points.
RL training yielded substantial improvements of 8.53 and 8.72 percentage points over the non-RL version, validating the progressive RL strategy's effectiveness.
AgeMem demonstrated higher Memory Quality (MQ) scores (0.533 for Qwen2.5, 0.605 for Qwen3) on HotpotQA and reduced prompt token usage by 3.1% to 5.1% compared to RAG-based counterparts.
solution: AgeMem formulates memory management as an intrinsic, learnable part of the agent's policy using a unified reinforcement learning (RL) framework.
Memory operations (e.g., store, retrieve, summarize, filter) are exposed as tool-based actions that the LLM agent can autonomously invoke.

A three-stage progressive RL strategy, combined with step-wise Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), is used to train the agent to learn stable and unified memory behaviors across different timescales, addressing sparse and discontinuous rewards.
paper_summary.summary: AgeMem introduces a framework for Large Language Model (LLM) agents that unifies long-term and short-term memory management through a learnable policy, enabling the agent to autonomously control memory operations. This approach improved average task performance across diverse benchmarks by 4.82 to 8.57 percentage points over existing memory-augmented baselines and enhanced memory quality and context efficiency.

title: Agentic Memory: Learning Unified Long-Term and Short-Term Memory Management for Large Language Model Agents

abstract: We introduce Agentic Reasoning, a framework that enhances large language model (LLM) reasoning by integrating external tool-using agents. Agentic Reasoning dynamically leverages web search, code execution, and structured memory to address complex problems requiring deep research. A key innovation in our framework is the Mind-Map agent, which constructs a structured knowledge graph to store reasoning context and track logical relationships, ensuring coherence in long reasoning chains with extensive tool usage. Additionally, we conduct a comprehensive exploration of the Web-Search agent, leading to a highly effective search mechanism that surpasses all prior approaches. When deployed on DeepSeek-R1, our method achieves a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) among public models and delivers performance comparable to OpenAI Deep Research, the leading proprietary model in this domain. Extensive ablation studies validate the optimal selection of agentic tools and confirm the effectiveness of our Mind-Map and Web-Search agents in enhancing LLM reasoning. The code is at: this https URL

keyInsights: Integrating a small, systematically selected set of agentic tools (Web-Search, Code, Mind-Map) through careful design yields superior performance compared to simply adding more tools.
A structured external memory, like the Mind-Map's knowledge graph, is crucial for maintaining coherence, tracking complex logical relationships, and mitigating errors in long reasoning chains.
Delegating specific tasks to specialized LLM-based agents, rather than direct API calls, enhances robustness, overcomes token limitations, and improves accuracy on difficult problems.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with complex, multi-step, knowledge-intensive reasoning in unstructured or open-ended domains, often producing rigid or flawed results.
Existing multi-agent LLM frameworks often lack optimized task delegation, structured integration, and struggle with maintaining coherence over long reasoning chains.
A performance gap exists between powerful proprietary LLMs with advanced tool-use capabilities and accessible open-source models, whose methodologies are often undisclosed.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on the GPQA dataset (PhD-level science QA) with 81.2% accuracy, surpassing OpenAI o3-mini-high (79.7%) and improving base DeepSeek-R1 by nearly 10%.
Set a new SOTA among publicly available methods on the GAIA benchmark with 66.13% average accuracy, outperforming OpenAI Deep Research on Level 1 and 2 tasks.
Significantly outperformed RAG and search-based methods on the FreshWiki dataset for Wikipedia article generation, achieving ROUGE-1 of 54.10 compared to STORM's 47.93.
solution: A streamlined framework called "Agentic Reasoning" dynamically integrates three universally effective external LLM-based agents: Web-Search, Code Execution, and a Mind-Map memory agent.
The primary reasoning LLM (DeepSeek-R1) intelligently invokes these agents by embedding specialized tokens and queries into its reasoning sequence, with agent outputs seamlessly reintegrated.
A novel Mind-Map agent creates a structured knowledge graph from reasoning chains, providing organized external memory, context clustering, and queryable knowledge for improved coherence.
paper_summary.summary: The "Agentic Reasoning" framework enhances Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning by integrating streamlined external agents for web-search, code execution, and a novel Mind-Map memory. The approach enables open-source LLMs to achieve state-of-the-art performance, rivalling or surpassing proprietary models on complex expert-level problem-solving and deep research tasks.

title: Agentic Reasoning: A Streamlined Framework for Enhancing LLM Reasoning with Agentic Tools

abstract: Memory is critical for AI agents, yet the widely-adopted static memory, aiming to create readily available memory in advance, is inevitably subject to severe information loss. To address this limitation, we propose a novel framework called \textbf{general agentic memory (GAM)}. GAM follows the principle of "\textbf{just-in time (JIT) compilation}" where it focuses on creating optimized contexts for its client at runtime while keeping only simple but useful memory during the offline stage. To this end, GAM employs a duo-design with the following components. 1) \textbf{Memorizer}, which highlights key historical information using a lightweight memory, while maintaining complete historical information within a universal page-store. 2) \textbf{Researcher}, which retrieves and integrates useful information from the page-store for its online request guided by the pre-constructed memory. This design allows GAM to effectively leverage the agentic capabilities and test-time scalability of frontier large language models (LLMs), while also facilitating end-to-end performance optimization through reinforcement learning. In our experimental study, we demonstrate that GAM achieves substantial improvement on various memory-grounded task completion scenarios against existing memory systems.

keyInsights: The Just-in-Time (JIT) compilation paradigm, which preserves full historical data and dynamically generates context, is effective in mitigating information loss observed in Ahead-of-Time (AOT) memory systems.
Leveraging frontier LLMs for an iterative "Researcher" module, involving planning, multi-tool searching, and self-reflection, enables sophisticated, multi-step reasoning capabilities for AI agents.
A lightweight, pre-computed memory serves a critical role in guiding the online deep research process, ensuring efficient and targeted retrieval from the comprehensive historical data.
originalProblem: Current AI agent memory systems, based on Ahead-of-Time (AOT) compilation, inevitably lose information during pre-computation and compression.
These AOT systems possess static memory structures, limiting their adaptability to unforeseen or fine-grained information requests at runtime.
Existing approaches often struggle with complex, multi-step information retrieval and reasoning across large or dispersed contexts, a problem exacerbated in basic RAG and long-context LLMs.
results: GAM consistently surpassed all baseline memory-free and state-of-the-art memory systems, exhibiting particular strength in complex, multi-step reasoning tasks where it achieved over 90% accuracy on HotpotQA and RULER multi-hop tracing.

The system's performance scaled positively with increased reflection depth and number of retrieved pages, confirming its ability to benefit from dynamic test-time computation and the efficacy of using a multi-tool search approach.

Ablation studies confirmed the indispensable roles of both the Memorizer's light memory in guiding search and the Researcher's deep-research capabilities for successful task completion, as removing either significantly degraded performance.
solution: Introduces General Agentic Memory (GAM), a framework based on the "Just-in-Time (JIT) Compilation" principle that preserves complete historical data in a "page-store."
Employs a dual-agent architecture: a Memorizer for offline processing to store full history and create light memory, and a Researcher for online, iterative "deep research."

The Researcher dynamically constructs optimized contexts by planning, searching across a multi-tool toolkit (embedding, BM25, ID-based retrievers) over the page-store, and reflecting on integrated results.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from BAAI, Peking University, Renmin University of China, and Hong Kong Polytechnic University developed General Agentic Memory (GAM), a framework for AI agents that employs a Just-in-Time Compilation principle through a dual-agent (Memorizer and Researcher) architecture. This approach, designed for dynamic context creation, consistently surpassed existing memory systems and achieved over 90% accuracy on complex multi-step reasoning benchmarks like HotpotQA.

title: General Agentic Memory Via Deep Research

abstract: Agent memory shapes how Large Language Model (LLM)-powered agents, akin to the human brain, progressively refine themselves through environment interactions. Existing paradigms remain constrained: parametric memory forcibly adjusts model parameters, and retrieval-based memory externalizes experience into structured databases, yet neither captures the fluid interweaving of reasoning and memory that underlies human cognition. To address this gap, we propose MemGen, a dynamic generative memory framework that equips agents with a human-esque cognitive faculty. It consists of a \textit{memory trigger}, which monitors the agent's reasoning state to decide explicit memory invocation, and a \textit{memory weaver}, which takes the agent's current state as stimulus to construct a latent token sequence as machine-native memory to enrich its reasoning. In this way, MemGen enables agents to recall and augment latent memory throughout reasoning, producing a tightly interwoven cycle of memory and cognition. Extensive experiments across eight benchmarks show that MemGen surpasses leading external memory systems such as ExpeL and AWM by up to $38.22\%$, exceeds GRPO by up to $13.44\%$, and exhibits strong cross-domain generalization ability. More importantly, we find that without explicit supervision, MemGen spontaneously evolves distinct human-like memory faculties, including planning memory, procedural memory, and working memory, suggesting an emergent trajectory toward more naturalistic forms of machine cognition.

keyInsights: The framework enables a seamless, reconstructive interweaving of memory and reasoning, moving beyond static retrieval or fixed parameter updates.
MemGen's modular design allows experiential knowledge to be internalized solely into the Weaver's parameters, preserving the core LLM's general capabilities and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
The system spontaneously evolves human-like memory hierarchies, including planning, procedural, and working memory, without explicit supervision, indicating emergent cognitive faculties.
originalProblem: Existing parametric memory in LLM agents suffers from catastrophic forgetting, erasing prior knowledge when adapting to new data.
Retrieval-based memory, while avoiding forgetting, lacks fluid integration with reasoning, relying on context engineering and rigid execution.
Current latent memory approaches often still rely on invasive parameter updates or primarily similarity-based retrieval, failing to capture dynamic, reconstructive memory processes.
results: MemGen achieved substantial performance gains, such as a 44.64% improvement over vanilla models on ALFWorld and a 27.06% gain on KodCode, across diverse domains.
Demonstrated strong cross-domain generalization, with memory learned on one dataset (e.g., KodCode) boosting performance on out-of-domain tasks like MATH (+17.6%).

Effectively mitigated catastrophic forgetting in continual learning settings, maintaining stable performance on old tasks while improving on new ones, and showed low per-query inference delays despite performance gains.
solution: MemGen proposes a dynamic generative latent memory framework with two LoRA adapters: a Memory Trigger and a Memory Weaver.
The Memory Trigger, trained with reinforcement learning, acts as a metacognitive monitor to intelligently decide when to invoke memory based on the reasoner's internal state.
The Memory Weaver dynamically synthesizes bespoke latent memories, which are seamlessly integrated into the frozen core LLM's reasoning process, preventing catastrophic forgetting.
paper_summary.summary: MemGen introduces a dynamic generative latent memory framework for large language model agents, allowing them to interweave memory synthesis with reasoning processes without altering the core model. This framework improves agent performance by up to 44.64% on tasks like ALFWorld and effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting, fostering the emergence of human-like memory hierarchies such as planning and procedural memory.

title: MemGen: Weaving Generative Latent Memory for Self-Evolving Agents

abstract: To support long-term interaction in complex environments, LLM agents require memory systems that manage historical experiences. Existing approaches either retain full interaction histories via passive context extension, leading to substantial redundancy, or rely on iterative reasoning to filter noise, incurring high token costs. To address this challenge, we introduce SimpleMem, an efficient memory framework based on semantic lossless compression. We propose a three-stage pipeline designed to maximize information density and token utilization: (1) Semantic Structured Compression, which distills unstructured interactions into compact, multi-view indexed memory units; (2) Online Semantic Synthesis, an intra-session process that instantly integrates related context into unified abstract representations to eliminate redundancy; and (3) Intent-Aware Retrieval Planning, which infers search intent to dynamically determine retrieval scope and construct precise context efficiently. Experiments on benchmark datasets show that our method consistently outperforms baseline approaches in accuracy, retrieval efficiency, and inference cost, achieving an average F1 improvement of 26.4% in LoCoMo while reducing inference-time token consumption by up to 30-fold, demonstrating a superior balance between performance and efficiency. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Efficient lifelong memory for LLM agents can be achieved by actively filtering noise and semantically compressing information, rather than merely extending context windows.
Structuring memory with multi-view indexing and recursive consolidation allows for scalable storage and retrieval of both fine-grained details and high-level abstract representations.
Dynamically adapting memory retrieval based on query complexity is crucial for maximizing token utilization and maintaining performance under strict efficiency constraints.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with fixed context windows, limiting their long-term memory and ability to retain information across extended interactions.
Memory systems for LLM agents suffer from context inflation due to redundant, low-entropy dialogue, which degrades effective information density and model performance.
Current approaches for augmenting LLM memory incur substantial computational overhead, including high token costs and increased latency, hindering practical deployment.
results: Achieved a 26.4% average F1 score improvement over strong baselines like Mem0 on the LoCoMo benchmark.
Reduced inference-time token consumption by up to 30x compared to full-context methods and 40-50% against other optimized baselines, significantly lowering operational costs.
Demonstrated superior efficiency with 14x faster memory construction and approximately 33% faster retrieval latency compared to leading baselines.
Enabled smaller LLMs (e.g., Qwen2.5-1.5b) to achieve competitive performance, outperforming larger models using less efficient memory strategies.
solution: A three-stage memory pipeline that includes semantic structured compression, multi-view structured indexing with recursive consolidation, and adaptive query-aware retrieval.
Implements entropy-aware filtering to remove redundant dialogue and normalizes informative content into self-contained, context-independent memory units.
Utilizes multi-layered indexing (semantic, lexical, symbolic) and an asynchronous background process for recursive consolidation, creating higher-level abstract memory representations.
Dynamically adjusts the number of retrieved memory entries based on an estimated query complexity, balancing context relevance and token efficiency.
paper_summary.summary: SimpleMem presents an efficient lifelong memory framework for LLM agents, utilizing semantic structured compression and adaptive retrieval to filter redundancy and consolidate experiences. The system achieves an average F1 score improvement of 26.4% while reducing inference-time token consumption by up to 30x and speeding up memory construction 14x compared to existing methods.

title: SimpleMem: Efficient Lifelong Memory for LLM Agents

abstract: Self-evolving memory systems are unprecedentedly reshaping the evolutionary paradigm of large language model (LLM)-based agents. Prior work has predominantly relied on manually engineered memory architectures to store trajectories, distill experience, and synthesize reusable tools, enabling agents to evolve on the fly within environment interactions. However, this paradigm is fundamentally constrained by the staticity of the memory system itself: while memory facilitates agent-level evolving, the underlying memory architecture cannot be meta-adapted to diverse task contexts. To address this gap, we propose MemEvolve, a meta-evolutionary framework that jointly evolves agents' experiential knowledge and their memory architecture, allowing agent systems not only to accumulate experience but also to progressively refine how they learn from it. To ground MemEvolve in prior research and foster openness in future self-evolving systems, we introduce EvolveLab, a unified self-evolving memory codebase that distills twelve representative memory systems into a modular design space (encode, store, retrieve, manage), providing both a standardized implementation substrate and a fair experimental arena. Extensive evaluations on four challenging agentic benchmarks demonstrate that MemEvolve achieves (I) substantial performance gains, improving frameworks such as SmolAgent and Flash-Searcher by up to $17.06\%$; and (II) strong cross-task and cross-LLM generalization, designing memory architectures that transfer effectively across diverse benchmarks and backbone models.

keyInsights: Transitioning LLM agents from 'skillful' to 'adaptive' learners requires evolving not just experience, but the memory architecture itself.
Memory systems can be effectively decomposed into modular, interdependent components (Encode, Store, Retrieve, Manage) to enable systematic meta-evolution.
Multi-objective feedback (performance, cost, delay) can guide the principled evolution of memory architectures, leading to more efficient and effective designs.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agents employ static memory architectures, limiting their adaptability to diverse tasks and environments.
Current self-improving agents act as 'skillful learners' with a fixed learning strategy but cannot dynamically refine *how* they learn.
No single memory architecture is universally optimal, leading to suboptimal performance and restricted generalizability in open-ended settings.
results: MemEvolve consistently delivered performance improvements of 3.54% to 5.0% across all benchmarks, with peak gains of up to 17.06% for certain agent frameworks.

Demonstrated strong generalization capabilities, as memory systems evolved on one task/LLM/framework transferred effectively to unseen contexts, enhancing performance for new tasks, different LLMs (Kimi K2, DeepSeek V3.2), and other agent frameworks.
Achieved these performance gains with comparable API costs and execution delays to baseline systems, indicating efficient architectural evolution.
solution: Introduced a meta-evolutionary framework, MemEvolve, for joint evolution of agents' experiential knowledge and their underlying memory architecture.
Developed EvolveLab, a unified codebase defining a modular design space (Encode, Store, Retrieve, Manage) for memory systems, making architecture evolution tractable.
Implemented a 'Diagnose-and-Design' outer loop that selects, diagnoses defects, and generates new memory architectures based on empirical feedback (performance, cost, delay).
paper_summary.summary: OPPO AI Agent Team and LV-NUS lab developed MemEvolve, a meta-evolutionary framework that allows large language model agents to adaptively refine their memory architectures. This approach enables agents to consistently improve task performance by up to 17.06% and generalize across various tasks, LLMs, and agent frameworks.

title: MemEvolve: Meta-Evolution of Agent Memory Systems

abstract: Modern language agents must operate over long-horizon, multi-turn interactions, where they retrieve external information, adapt to observations, and answer interdependent queries. Yet, most LLM systems rely on full-context prompting, appending all past turns regardless of their relevance. This leads to unbounded memory growth, increased computational costs, and degraded reasoning performance on out-of-distribution input lengths. We introduce MEM1, an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework that enables agents to operate with constant memory across long multi-turn tasks. At each turn, MEM1 updates a compact shared internal state that jointly supports memory consolidation and reasoning. This state integrates prior memory with new observations from the environment while strategically discarding irrelevant or redundant information. To support training in more realistic and compositional settings, we propose a simple yet effective and scalable approach to constructing multi-turn environments by composing existing datasets into arbitrarily complex task sequences. Experiments across three domains, including internal retrieval QA, open-domain web QA, and multi-turn web shopping, show that MEM1-7B improves performance by 3.5x while reducing memory usage by 3.7x compared to Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct on a 16-objective multi-hop QA task, and generalizes beyond the training horizon. Our results demonstrate the promise of reasoning-driven memory consolidation as a scalable alternative to existing solutions for training long-horizon interactive agents, where both efficiency and performance are optimized.

keyInsights: Inference-time reasoning can be synergized with memory consolidation, allowing the agent to learn to extract and retain key information while discarding irrelevant details.
Reinforcement learning, when combined with context pruning, naturally incentivizes the agent to develop effective internal memory management strategies.
Composing existing single-objective datasets into multi-objective tasks creates effective long-horizon benchmarks for training and evaluating memory-efficient agents.
originalProblem: Unbounded context growth in multi-turn LLM interactions leads to exploding computational costs and memory usage (O(N^2) or O(N)).
LLMs trained on short contexts struggle to generalize to longer, out-of-distribution input lengths, causing performance degradation.
Accumulating irrelevant information in the context overloads the model's attention, hindering reasoning and leading to the "lost in the middle" problem.
results: Maintains an almost constant peak token count on multi-objective QA tasks while other methods show linear growth, achieving up to 72.9% reduction in peak tokens.
Surpasses larger LLMs (e.g., Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct) in performance on complex 8- and 16-objective QA tasks, showing better generalization to long horizons.
Achieves the highest average final reward (70.87) on WebShop navigation with significantly lower resource usage (2.8x lower peak tokens, 1.5x faster inference) compared to baselines.
solution: Introduces a compact shared internal state (<IS>) that iteratively consolidates prior memory with new observations, becoming the agent's only retained memory.
Prunes all previous turn context, tool outputs, and observations after consolidation, maintaining constant memory usage across turns.
Trains the agent end-to-end using reinforcement learning (PPO) where task success incentivizes the agent to learn memory consolidation intrinsically.
paper_summary.summary: MEM1 proposes an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework that enables Large Language Model agents to maintain constant memory usage while operating over long-horizon, multi-turn interactions. This framework, from researchers at SMART/MIT, demonstrates superior efficiency and competitive or improved performance on multi-objective QA and interactive navigation tasks.

title: MEM1: Learning to Synergize Memory and Reasoning for Efficient Long-Horizon Agents

abstract: Procedural memory enables large language model (LLM) agents to internalize "how-to" knowledge, theoretically reducing redundant trial-and-error. However, existing frameworks predominantly suffer from a "passive accumulation" paradigm, treating memory as a static append-only archive. To bridge the gap between static storage and dynamic reasoning, we propose $\textbf{ReMe}$ ($\textit{Remember Me, Refine Me}$), a comprehensive framework for experience-driven agent evolution. ReMe innovates across the memory lifecycle via three mechanisms: 1) $\textit{multi-faceted distillation}$, which extracts fine-grained experiences by recognizing success patterns, analyzing failure triggers and generating comparative insights; 2) $\textit{context-adaptive reuse}$, which tailors historical insights to new contexts via scenario-aware indexing; and 3) $\textit{utility-based refinement}$, which autonomously adds valid memories and prunes outdated ones to maintain a compact, high-quality experience pool. Extensive experiments on BFCL-V3 and AppWorld demonstrate that ReMe establishes a new state-of-the-art in agent memory system. Crucially, we observe a significant memory-scaling effect: Qwen3-8B equipped with ReMe outperforms larger, memoryless Qwen3-14B, suggesting that self-evolving memory provides a computation-efficient pathway for lifelong learning. We release our code and the $\texttt{this http URL}$ dataset to facilitate further research.

keyInsights: Procedural memory for LLM agents is more effective when dynamic and feedback-driven, rather than a passive, append-only archive.
The quality and granularity of distilled experiences, specifically 'keypoint-level' information, are more crucial for knowledge transfer than raw, full trajectories.
Smaller LLMs, when augmented with a sophisticated dynamic memory framework, can match or exceed the performance of significantly larger, memory-less models, demonstrating a 'memory-scaling effect'.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent procedural memory systems passively accumulate raw data, leading to coarse-grained information and a lack of adaptive application of past experiences.
The absence of dynamic update strategies causes memory pools to accumulate outdated or unhelpful information, degrading performance over time.
Agents struggle to efficiently internalize 'how-to' knowledge, resulting in redundant trial-and-error and limited continuous improvement.
results: ReMe consistently achieved state-of-the-art performance across BFCL-V3 and AppWorld benchmarks, with Qwen3-8B + ReMe (dynamic) yielding average gains of 8.83% in Avg@4 and 7.29% in Pass@4 over a 'No Memory' baseline.
The framework demonstrated a memory-scaling effect, where Qwen3-8B + ReMe (dynamic) outperformed the vanilla Qwen3-14B model, and Qwen3-14B + ReMe (dynamic) surpassed the vanilla Qwen3-32B model.

Dynamic refinement components (selective addition, failure-aware reflection, utility-based deletion) were shown to be crucial, with keypoint-level experiences and LLM-generated 'usage scenarios' proving most effective for indexing and retrieval.
solution: ReMe implements a closed-loop system of experience acquisition, reuse, and refinement for LLM agents.
Experience acquisition distills high-quality, structured 'keypoint-level' knowledge from multi-faceted analyses of both successful and failed trajectories.

Experience reuse involves retrieving relevant insights, adaptively reranking, and rewriting them to provide task-specific guidance; refinement continuously optimizes memory through selective addition, failure-aware reflection, and utility-based deletion.
paper_summary.summary: ReMe introduces a dynamic procedural memory framework for LLM agents, enabling continuous learning and adaptation through a closed-loop system of experience acquisition, reuse, and refinement. This framework allows smaller language models to achieve performance comparable to or surpassing larger, memory-less models, demonstrating a memory-scaling effect and improving agent robustness.

title: Remember Me, Refine Me: A Dynamic Procedural Memory Framework for Experience-Driven Agent Evolution

abstract: Memory systems have been designed to leverage past experiences in Large Language Model (LLM) agents. However, many deployed memory systems primarily optimize compression and storage, with comparatively less emphasis on explicit, closed-loop control of memory retrieval. From this observation, we build memory retrieval as an autonomous, accurate, and compatible agent system, named MemR$^3$, which has two core mechanisms: 1) a router that selects among retrieve, reflect, and answer actions to optimize answer quality; 2) a global evidence-gap tracker that explicitly renders the answering process transparent and tracks the evidence collection process. This design departs from the standard retrieve-then-answer pipeline by introducing a closed-loop control mechanism that enables autonomous decision-making. Empirical results on the LoCoMo benchmark demonstrate that MemR$^3$ surpasses strong baselines on LLM-as-a-Judge score, and particularly, it improves existing retrievers across four categories with an overall improvement on RAG (+7.29%) and Zep (+1.94%) using GPT-4.1-mini backend, offering a plug-and-play controller for existing memory stores.

keyInsights: Maintaining an explicit "evidence-gap" state allows agents to reason under partial observability and dynamically refine their information-seeking strategy.
Transforming memory retrieval into a closed-loop, autonomous sequential decision process significantly enhances the accuracy and efficiency of LLM agents.
A plug-and-play retrieval controller can be integrated with diverse memory backends (e.g., RAG, Zep) to improve retrieval quality without modifying storage architecture.
originalProblem: Non-parametric memory systems for LLM agents often rely on heuristic, open-loop retrieval, leading to noisy recall, excessive context, and latency issues.
LLM agents lack explicit, closed-loop control mechanisms for memory retrieval, limiting their ability to intelligently gather specific information.
Managing and leveraging long-term, personalized memories remains a significant challenge for sophisticated AI agents.
results: MemR^3 improved LLM-as-a-Judge (J) scores by up to 7.29% (RAG-backed, GPT-4.1-mini) on the LoCoMo benchmark, consistently outperforming its base retrievers and baselines.
The system demonstrated substantial gains across multi-hop, temporal, and open-domain questions, approaching or surpassing full-context performance in several categories.
Ablation studies highlighted the crucial role of "masked retrieval" (preventing redundant retrieval) and the positive impact of explicit reflection and query refinement.
solution: MemR^3 frames memory retrieval as an autonomous, sequential decision process within a LangGraph-based agent graph.
It introduces a global evidence-gap tracker (E, G) to explicitly monitor known facts and remaining informational needs, guiding retrieval.
A Router node provides closed-loop control, dynamically orchestrating actions between Retrieve, Reflect, and Answer nodes to refine knowledge.
paper_summary.summary: MemR^3 introduces a system for LLM agents that uses reflective reasoning and an explicit evidence-gap tracker to enable closed-loop control over memory retrieval. This approach consistently enhanced answer quality, achieving up to a 7.29% improvement in LLM-as-a-Judge scores on the LoCoMo benchmark compared to traditional RAG.

title: MemR$^3$: Memory Retrieval via Reflective Reasoning for LLM Agents

abstract: The evolution of recommender systems has shifted preference storage from rating matrices and dense embeddings to semantic memory in the agentic era. Yet existing agents rely on isolated memory, overlooking crucial collaborative signals. Bridging this gap is hindered by the dual challenges of distilling vast graph contexts without overwhelming reasoning agents with cognitive load, and evolving the collaborative memory efficiently without incurring prohibitive computational costs. To address this, we propose MemRec, a framework that architecturally decouples reasoning from memory management to enable efficient collaborative augmentation. MemRec introduces a dedicated, cost-effective LM_Mem to manage a dynamic collaborative memory graph, serving synthesized, high-signal context to a downstream LLM_Rec. The framework operates via a practical pipeline featuring efficient retrieval and cost-effective asynchronous graph propagation that evolves memory in the background. Extensive experiments on four benchmarks demonstrate that MemRec achieves state-of-the-art performance. Furthermore, architectural analysis confirms its flexibility, establishing a new Pareto frontier that balances reasoning quality, cost, and privacy through support for diverse deployments, including local open-source models. Code:this https URL and Homepage: this https URL

keyInsights: Integrating dynamic collaborative memory into LLM-powered agents, rather than relying on isolated memory, significantly enhances recommendation quality by leveraging global graph signals.
Architectural decoupling of memory management and reasoning, combined with LLM-guided context curation, is effective in preventing cognitive overload for the reasoning agent.
Asynchronous, batched propagation of memory updates provides an efficient and scalable solution for dynamically evolving a collaborative memory graph in agentic recommender systems.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-powered agentic recommender systems rely on an isolated memory paradigm, failing to leverage crucial global collaborative signals like community trends and serendipitous discoveries.

Directly feeding extensive collaborative neighborhood information to LLM agents leads to cognitive overload, impairing their ability to distill pertinent information and increasing the risk of hallucinations.
Maintaining and dynamically updating a collaborative memory graph with LLM agents is computationally intractable due to prohibitive synchronous update costs, creating a significant bottleneck.
results: MemRec consistently outperformed all traditional and agentic baselines, achieving state-of-the-art results with substantial gains, including a +28.98% relative improvement in H@1 on Goodreads and +15.77% on Yelp compared to the strongest isolated memory agents.

Architectural decoupling proved crucial; MemRec's approach yielded a +34% relative improvement in H@1 on Books compared to a 'Naive Collaborative Agent' that suffered from cognitive overload due to processing uncurated context.

The system established a new Pareto frontier balancing performance and cost, enabling flexible and cost-effective deployment options, and demonstrating high token efficiency due to strategic input-biased memory operations.
solution: MemRec proposes an architecturally decoupled framework with a dedicated Memory Manager (LM_Mem) handling memory dynamics and a Reasoning Core (LLM_Rec) focusing on recommendations.

It employs a 'Curate-then-Synthesize' strategy, utilizing a zero-shot LLM-as-Rule-Generator for efficient context curation and LM_Mem for synthesizing concise collaborative memory (M_collab) to prevent cognitive overload.

An Asynchronous Collaborative Propagation mechanism updates user and item memories in batched operations, ensuring continuous graph evolution with O(1) interaction complexity per recommendation, thereby avoiding computational bottlenecks.
paper_summary.summary: MemRec introduces a collaborative memory-augmented agentic recommender system that integrates global collaborative signals into LLM-powered agents. The system achieves state-of-the-art performance across four diverse benchmarks, demonstrating substantial improvements, such as a +28.98% relative gain in H@1 on Goodreads, by overcoming cognitive overload and computational challenges through a decoupled architecture.

title: MemRec: Collaborative Memory-Augmented Agentic Recommender System

abstract: LLM-based agents have been extensively applied across various domains, where memory stands out as one of their most essential capabilities. Previous memory mechanisms of LLM-based agents are manually predefined by human experts, leading to higher labor costs and suboptimal performance. In addition, these methods overlook the memory cycle effect in interactive scenarios, which is critical to optimizing LLM-based agents for specific environments. To address these challenges, in this paper, we propose to optimize LLM-based agents with an adaptive and data-driven memory framework by modeling memory cycles. Specifically, we design an MoE gate function to facilitate memory retrieval, propose a learnable aggregation process to improve memory utilization, and develop task-specific reflection to adapt memory storage. Our memory framework empowers LLM-based agents to learn how to memorize information effectively in specific environments, with both off-policy and on-policy optimization. In order to evaluate the effectiveness of our proposed methods, we conduct comprehensive experiments across multiple aspects. To benefit the research community in this area, we release our project at this https URL.

keyInsights: The "memory cycle" demonstrates that memory storage, retrieval, and utilization procedures are mutually influential and require joint optimization for effective agent performance.
On-policy optimization is essential for effectively training adaptive memory systems, as it mitigates distribution shifts that can hinder purely off-policy approaches.

Data-driven, learnable components for retrieval (adaptive metric weighting), utilization (iterative aggregation), and storage (task-specific reflection) enable LLM agents to autonomously adapt their memorization strategies.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent memory systems depend on manually predefined heuristics for retrieval, summarization, and storage, requiring extensive human tuning.
These fixed rules lead to suboptimal performance and struggle to generalize across diverse tasks and environments.
Prior work largely overlooked the interactive "memory cycle" where an agent's memory processes and environmental feedback are interdependent and should be jointly optimized.
results: The proposed framework with on-policy optimization achieved superior Exact Match accuracy across HotpotQA benchmarks (hard, medium, easy) using different LLM backbones (GPT-4o-mini, Qwen-2.5, Llama-3.1).
It significantly reduced the average number of reasoning steps and total time per trajectory, indicating improved task completion efficiency.

Ablation studies demonstrated that individually optimizing memory components or directly combining off-policy optimized parameters can degrade performance, underscoring the importance of joint optimization within the memory cycle.
solution: Introduce an adaptive, data-driven memory framework that optimizes LLM agents' memory processes (retrieval, utilization, storage).
Implement a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) gate for adaptive retrieval, a learnable aggregation process for utilization, and a task-specific self-reflection mechanism for storage.
Optimize the interdependent memory procedures using both off-policy (contrastive learning, SFT, DPO, self-reflection) and on-policy learning strategies.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Renmin University of China and Huawei Noah's Ark Lab developed an adaptive memory framework that enables Large Language Model (LLM) agents to learn optimized strategies for memory retrieval, utilization, and storage. This data-driven approach, which accounts for the memory cycle effect, leads to superior task performance and reduced reasoning steps in various question-answering tasks.

title: Learn to Memorize: Optimizing LLM-based Agents with Adaptive Memory Framework

abstract: Long-term memory is one of the key factors influencing the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Model Agents (LLM Agents). Incorporating a memory mechanism that effectively integrates past interactions can significantly enhance decision-making and contextual coherence of LLM Agents. While recent works have made progress in memory storage and retrieval, such as encoding memory into dense vectors for similarity-based search or organizing knowledge in the form of graph, these approaches often fall short in structured memory organization and efficient retrieval. To address these limitations, we propose a Hierarchical Memory (H-MEM) architecture for LLM Agents that organizes and updates memory in a multi-level fashion based on the degree of semantic abstraction. Each memory vector is embedded with a positional index encoding pointing to its semantically related sub-memories in the next layer. During the reasoning phase, an index-based routing mechanism enables efficient, layer-by-layer retrieval without performing exhaustive similarity computations. We evaluate our method on five task settings from the LoCoMo dataset. Experimental results show that our approach consistently outperforms five baseline methods, demonstrating its effectiveness in long-term dialogue scenarios.

keyInsights: Structuring memory hierarchically with embedded positional indices enables a targeted retrieval process that significantly reduces the search space and computational complexity compared to flat vector storage.

Dynamic adjustment of memory strength, incorporating user feedback, allows the memory system to adapt to evolving conversational contexts and user interests, reinforcing relevant memories and fading irrelevant ones.

The synergy between hierarchical organization and index-based retrieval is critical for scalable and efficient long-term reasoning in LLM agents, offering a robust alternative to purely vector-based or complex graph-based memory systems.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) suffer from limited context windows, making it challenging to maintain coherence and context over extended, multi-turn interactions.

Existing memory mechanisms for LLM agents often rely on inefficient retrieval methods, such as exhaustive similarity searches across a growing memory store, leading to high computational costs and slow inference.

Many current memory systems lack a structured organization, hindering precise information localization and integration, and use simplistic update mechanisms that do not adapt to dynamic user interests or feedback.
results: H-MEM consistently achieved superior performance, demonstrating average F1 score improvements of 14.98 points and BLEU-1 score improvements of 12.77 points over baseline methods on the LoCoMo dataset.

The architecture exhibited significant computational efficiency, maintaining inference times below 100 ms even at maximum memory load, which is up to 5 times faster than vector-based baselines like MemoryBank.

H-MEM showed particularly strong performance in complex tasks, outperforming baselines by 21.25 points in F1 for Multi-Hop questions and 16.71 points in F1 for Adversarial tasks, maintaining effectiveness across various LLM scales.
solution: H-MEM introduces a four-level hierarchical memory architecture (Domain, Category, Memory Trace, Episode Layers) that semantically organizes information, mirroring human cognitive processes.

An index-based routing mechanism is implemented for memory retrieval, where each memory entry includes positional indices pointing to related sub-memories, enabling efficient, top-down traversal without exhaustive searches.

A dynamic memory update mechanism adjusts memory strength based on LLM-generated feedback weights and explicit user feedback (approval, no feedback, rebuttal), enhancing adaptability beyond traditional forgetting curves.
paper_summary.summary: An H-MEM architecture enables Large Language Model agents to achieve high-efficiency long-term reasoning through a novel hierarchical memory structure and index-based retrieval mechanism. This system demonstrably improves F1 and BLEU-1 scores on complex long-term dialogue tasks while drastically reducing computational overhead compared to existing memory approaches.

title: Hierarchical Memory for High-Efficiency Long-Term Reasoning in LLM Agents

abstract: Autonomous agent frameworks still struggle to reconcile long-term experiential learning with real-time, context-sensitive decision-making. In practice, this gap appears as static cognition, rigid workflow dependence, and inefficient context usage, which jointly limit adaptability in open-ended and non-stationary environments. To address these limitations, we present AutoAgent, a self-evolving multi-agent framework built on three tightly coupled components: evolving cognition, on-the-fly contextual decision-making, and elastic memory orchestration. At the core of AutoAgent, each agent maintains structured prompt-level cognition over tools, self-capabilities, peer expertise, and task knowledge. During execution, this cognition is combined with live task context to select actions from a unified space that includes tool calls, LLM-based generation, and inter-agent requests. To support efficient long-horizon reasoning, an Elastic Memory Orchestrator dynamically organizes interaction history by preserving raw records, compressing redundant trajectories, and constructing reusable episodic abstractions, thereby reducing token overhead while retaining decision-critical evidence. These components are integrated through a closed-loop cognitive evolution process that aligns intended actions with observed outcomes to continuously update cognition and expand reusable skills, without external retraining. Empirical results across retrieval-augmented reasoning, tool-augmented agent benchmarks, and embodied task environments show that AutoAgent consistently improves task success, tool-use efficiency, and collaborative robustness over static and memory-augmented baselines. Overall, AutoAgent provides a unified and practical foundation for adaptive autonomous agents that must learn from experience while making reliable context-aware decisions in dynamic environments.

keyInsights: Dynamic cognition is essential for adaptability, allowing agents to continuously refine their understanding of tools, peers, and environments for robust performance in open-ended settings.

Contextual decision-making, which integrates self-driven (emic) and collaborative (etic) actions on the fly, provides flexibility for agents to adapt problem-solving strategies in real time, enhancing robustness to unexpected situations.

Efficient memory orchestration not only reduces token overhead but also enables the distillation of reusable skills from interaction history, fostering long-term knowledge acquisition and supporting long-horizon tasks.
originalProblem: Existing agents often struggle with static cognition, operating with fixed knowledge that cannot update through experience, leading to suboptimal decisions and tool misuse.
Many agent systems rely on rigid, pre-defined workflows, limiting their ability to adapt to novel situations or unexpected outcomes and preventing dynamic replanning.

Inefficient context and memory management, characterized by linear growth of interaction history, causes token redundancy, increased computational overhead, and difficulty in retrieving relevant information for long-term learning.
results: Achieved superior RAG performance, with the highest average accuracy (0.3965) and LLM-judge score (0.4315), establishing new state-of-the-art results on HotpotQA (Acc: 0.530), 2WikiMultihopQA (Acc: 0.466), and Bamboogle (Acc: 0.456).

Demonstrated enhanced performance on tool-augmented agent benchmarks, consistently outperforming baselines like ReAct and DeepAgent across GAIA, HLE-Bench, and ALFWorld, achieving up to 54.5% success on GAIA and 99.3% on ALFWorld with closed-source models.

Ablation studies confirmed the benefits of core components: EMO significantly improved performance (e.g., GAIA 'all' metric from 45.5% to 52.1%), and evolving cognition enhanced robustness under tool instability (e.g., web search F1 improved from 24.07% to 29.67%).
solution: Introduces a Self-Evolution Loop that enables continuous verification and update of agent knowledge (cognition, decision, memory, evolution) through operational practice.
Develops an Evolving Cognition Layer as a dynamic knowledge base, containing internal models (tools, skills) and external models (peers, environment), which are refined by interaction outcomes.
Implements an Elastic Memory Orchestrator (EMO) for efficient context management, dynamically compressing interaction history and distilling multi-step procedures into reusable skills.
paper_summary.summary: By integrating evolvable cognition, on-the-fly contextual decision-making, and elastic memory orchestration, AutoAgent enables autonomous agents to continuously adapt and learn from experience in dynamic environments. The framework achieves superior performance across RAG and tool-augmented agent benchmarks, establishing new state-of-the-art on datasets such as HotpotQA and Bamboogle, and boosting agent task success rates on GAIA and ALFWorld for diverse LLM backbones.

title: AutoAgent: Evolving Cognition and Elastic Memory Orchestration for Adaptive Agents

abstract: The rapid evolution of Large Language Model (LLM) agents has necessitated robust memory systems to support cohesive long-term interaction and complex reasoning. Benefiting from the strong capabilities of LLMs, recent research focus has shifted from simple context extension to the development of dedicated agentic memory systems. However, existing approaches typically rely on rigid retrieval granularity, accumulation-heavy maintenance strategies, and coarse-grained update mechanisms. These design choices create a persistent mismatch between stored information and task-specific reasoning demands, while leading to the unchecked accumulation of logical inconsistencies over time. To address these challenges, we propose Adaptive Memory via Multi-Agent Collaboration (AMA), a novel framework that leverages coordinated agents to manage memory across multiple granularities. AMA employs a hierarchical memory design that dynamically aligns retrieval granularity with task complexity. Specifically, the Constructor and Retriever jointly enable multi-granularity memory construction and adaptive query routing. The Judge verifies the relevance and consistency of retrieved content, triggering iterative retrieval when evidence is insufficient or invoking the Refresher upon detecting logical conflicts. The Refresher then enforces memory consistency by performing targeted updates or removing outdated entries. Extensive experiments on challenging long-context benchmarks show that AMA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines while reducing token consumption by approximately 80% compared to full-context methods, demonstrating its effectiveness in maintaining retrieval precision and long-term memory consistency.

keyInsights: Multi-agent collaboration can effectively decompose and manage complex memory challenges, leading to more robust and adaptive LLM agent behavior.
Storing information at multiple granularities and adaptively retrieving it based on task demands is crucial for overcoming the limitations of fixed-granularity memory systems.
Logic-driven verification and explicit consistency maintenance mechanisms are essential for preventing error accumulation and ensuring the long-term reliability of an agent's knowledge base.
originalProblem: Existing LLM memory systems struggle with fixed retrieval granularity, leading to information loss, irrelevant noise, or fragmented logical dependencies.
Prior memory maintenance strategies often accumulate redundancies and errors without robust mechanisms for precision or consistency over long interactions.
A lack of adaptive routing in current frameworks prevents dynamic selection of appropriate memory granularity based on task demands during inference.
results: AMA achieved superior performance on the LoCoMo Benchmark, with a GPT-4o-mini LLM Score of 0.774, surpassing Nemori (0.740), and outperformed `FullContext` for GPT-4.1-mini (0.805 vs. 0.786).
The framework demonstrated high accuracy on the LongMemEval_s Benchmark (0.698 average, 0.897 on `knowledge-update` tasks), outperforming baselines like Nemori (0.642).

AMA significantly reduced token consumption to approximately 19% compared to `FullContext` methods, demonstrating improved efficiency while maintaining or improving performance, and ablation studies confirmed the criticality of its multi-granularity and refresher components.
solution: A multi-agent framework composed of Constructor, Retriever, Judge, and Refresher agents manages the entire memory lifecycle for LLM agents.
A hierarchical memory design stores information at multi-granular levels: raw text, atomic facts, and abstract episodes.

An adaptive retrieval mechanism dynamically routes queries to the most appropriate memory granularity based on query intent, while the Judge and Refresher agents ensure consistency through verification and targeted updates.
paper_summary.summary: The AMA framework introduces an adaptive multi-agent memory system for LLM agents, dynamically managing memory granularity and ensuring consistency through specialized agents. This system achieved an overall LLM Score of 0.774 on the LoCoMo Benchmark with GPT-4o-mini, surpassing other memory-based methods, and reduced token consumption by approximately 81% compared to full-context approaches.

title: AMA: Adaptive Memory via Multi-Agent Collaboration

abstract: Memory-augmented LLM agents maintain external memory banks to support long-horizon interaction, yet most existing systems treat construction, retrieval, and utilization as isolated subroutines. This creates two coupled challenges: strategic blindness on the forward path of the memory cycle, where construction and retrieval are driven by local heuristics rather than explicit strategic reasoning, and sparse, delayed supervision on the backward path, where downstream failures rarely translate into direct repairs of the memory bank. To address these challenges, we propose MemMA, a plug-and-play multi-agent framework that coordinates the memory cycle along both the forward and backward paths. On the forward path, a Meta-Thinker produces structured guidance that steers a Memory Manager during construction and directs a Query Reasoner during iterative retrieval. On the backward path, MemMA introduces in-situ self-evolving memory construction, which synthesizes probe QA pairs, verifies the current memory, and converts failures into repair actions before the memory is finalized. Extensive experiments on LoCoMo show that MemMA consistently outperforms existing baselines across multiple LLM backbones and improves three different storage backends in a plug-and-play manner. Our code is publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Explicitly coordinating the entire memory cycle, rather than optimizing individual stages, is crucial for improving the performance of long-horizon LLM agents.
Strategic guidance from a 'Meta-Thinker' effectively mitigates 'strategic blindness' in memory construction (e.g., resolving conflicts) and enables more effective iterative retrieval.
Providing immediate, localized feedback and repair signals through 'in-situ self-evolution' can overcome the challenge of sparse and delayed credit assignment in memory maintenance.
originalProblem: Memory-augmented LLM agents often treat memory construction, retrieval, and utilization as isolated stages, leading to suboptimal performance due to "strategic blindness."
Myopic memory construction decisions result in redundant, contradictory, or incomplete memory banks, while aimless retrieval frequently fails to surface necessary evidence.
The backward path of the memory cycle suffers from sparse and delayed feedback, making it challenging to identify and repair specific memory construction errors that lead to later task failures.
results: MemMA improved overall accuracy by 5.92% (to 81.58% ACC) on the LoCoMo dataset when using GPT-4o-mini and the LightMem backend, significantly outperforming existing baselines.
The framework consistently enhanced the performance of three different memory storage backends (Single-Agent, A-Mem, LightMem), validating its plug-and-play adaptability across diverse systems.

Ablation studies confirmed that iterative retrieval (causing the largest drop) and in-situ self-evolution were the most critical components, affirming the benefits of coordinating both forward and backward memory cycle paths.
solution: MemMA introduces a plug-and-play multi-agent framework that coordinates the memory cycle along both its forward and backward paths, separating strategic reasoning from low-level execution.
The forward path employs a planner-worker architecture with a Meta-Thinker providing strategic guidance for memory construction and an iterative, diagnosis-guided Query Reasoner for retrieval.

The backward path features an in-situ self-evolution mechanism that generates synthetic probes after each conversational session to verify provisional memory, diagnose failures, and propose targeted repairs before memory is committed.
paper_summary.summary: MemMA is a multi-agent framework designed to coordinate the entire memory cycle for long-horizon LLM agents, enhancing memory construction and retrieval through reasoning-aware processes and in-situ self-evolution. This approach achieved an overall accuracy improvement of 5.92% with GPT-4o-mini on a conversational memory benchmark, demonstrating enhanced reliability in complex reasoning tasks.

title: MemMA: Coordinating the Memory Cycle through Multi-Agent Reasoning and In-Situ Self-Evolution

abstract: Self-evolving memory serves as the trainable parameters for Large Language Models (LLMs)-based agents, where extraction (distilling insights from experience) and management (updating the memory bank) must be tightly coordinated. Existing methods predominately optimize memory management while treating memory extraction as a static process, resulting in poor generalization, where agents accumulate instance-specific noise rather than robust memories. To address this, we propose Unified Memory Extraction and Management (UMEM), a self-evolving agent framework that jointly optimizes a Large Language Model to simultaneous extract and manage memories. To mitigate overfitting to specific instances, we introduce Semantic Neighborhood Modeling and optimize the model with a neighborhood-level marginal utility reward via GRPO. This approach ensures memory generalizability by evaluating memory utility across clusters of semantically related queries. Extensive experiments across five benchmarks demonstrate that UMEM significantly outperforms highly competitive baselines, achieving up to a 10.67% improvement in multi-turn interactive tasks. Futhermore, UMEM maintains a monotonic growth curve during continuous evolution. Codes and models will be publicly released.

keyInsights: Jointly optimizing memory extraction and management is critical for robust and generalizable self-evolving agents, addressing limitations of approaches that decouple these processes.
Evaluating memory utility not just on individual queries but across semantic neighborhoods is essential to prevent overfitting to specific instances and ensure generalizable knowledge acquisition.
The quality of extracted memory is paramount for effective self-evolution, with optimization of extraction having a significantly greater impact on performance than management optimization alone.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based agents struggle with continuous learning and adaptation due to frozen parameters and issues with non-parametric external memory.
Current memory systems often fall into "rote memorization," accumulating instance-specific details rather than abstract, generalizable principles, leading to "memory pollution."

There is a fundamental misalignment between statically extracted, low-quality memories and sophisticated memory management policies, undermining effective self-evolution and cross-task generalization.
results: UMEM consistently outperformed state-of-the-art baselines, achieving up to a 10.67% improvement in multi-turn interactive tasks (ALFWorld) and showing superior performance across five diverse benchmarks.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical role of Semantic Neighborhood Modeling (preventing a ~10% performance drop) and highlighted that optimizing memory extraction led to a substantially larger performance gain than optimizing management.

UMEM demonstrated superior stability and monotonic performance growth in long-horizon self-evolution settings, accumulating fewer harmful memories and leading to more efficient decision-making with reduced average steps to task completion.
solution: UMEM introduces a unified framework that jointly optimizes both memory extraction (what to store) and memory management (how to use/update) through a dedicated "Mem-Optimizer."
Semantic Neighborhood Modeling (SNM) is employed to evaluate candidate memory updates across clusters of semantically related queries, forcing the extraction of generalizable insights.

The Mem-Optimizer is trained using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and guided by a novel Marginal Utility Reward, which combines success gain and efficiency regularization to promote effective and generalizable learning.
paper_summary.summary: The UMEM framework jointly optimizes memory extraction and management for Large Language Model (LLM) agents, enabling continuous learning and adaptation in dynamic environments. It leverages Semantic Neighborhood Modeling and Group Relative Policy Optimization to ensure memories are generalizable, leading to up to 10.67% performance improvement in multi-turn interactive tasks and more stable self-evolution compared to existing methods.

title: UMEM: Unified Memory Extraction and Management Framework for Generalizable Memory

abstract: Large language model-based agents operating in long-horizon interactions require memory systems that support temporal consistency, multi-hop reasoning, and evidence-grounded reuse across sessions. Existing approaches largely rely on unstructured retrieval or coarse abstractions, which often lead to temporal conflicts, brittle reasoning, and limited traceability. We propose MemWeaver, a unified memory framework that consolidates long-term agent experiences into three interconnected components: a temporally grounded graph memory for structured relational reasoning, an experience memory that abstracts recurring interaction patterns from repeated observations, and a passage memory that preserves original textual evidence. MemWeaver employs a dual-channel retrieval strategy that jointly retrieves structured knowledge and supporting evidence to construct compact yet information-dense contexts for reasoning. Experiments on the LoCoMo benchmark demonstrate that MemWeaver substantially improves multi-hop and temporal reasoning accuracy while reducing input context length by over 95\% compared to long-context baselines.

organization_info.name: The University of New South Wales

keyInsights: Effective long-term agent memory requires an active *consolidation process* that transforms episodic interaction traces into structured, reusable, and verifiable knowledge, rather than just passive storage.

Integrating distinct memory types (structured facts, abstracted experiences, raw passages) within a unified framework, with explicit links and systematic maintenance, provides synergistic benefits for complex, long-horizon reasoning.
Explicit temporal grounding within a structured knowledge graph is crucial for accurate temporal consistency and compositional reasoning in dynamic interactive settings.
originalProblem: LLM agents struggle with temporal consistency, multi-hop reasoning, and accumulating user-specific knowledge over long, multi-session interactions due to limited context windows.

Existing memory systems are often insufficient, either lacking relational/temporal structure (flat retrieval), suffering from noisy extractions and lack of temporal grounding (KGs), or lacking traceability to original evidence (abstraction-based).
Many current agentic memory frameworks offer limited support for fine-grained temporal reasoning, evidence-grounded correction, and systematic consolidation of memory over time.
results: MemWeaver consistently outperformed baselines on the LoCoMo dataset, showing substantial improvements in reasoning-intensive tasks; for instance, Temporal F1 with GPT-4o-mini boosted from 38.77 (A-Mem) to 50.83.

Achieved significant token efficiency, reducing inference-time context length by over 95% (staying within 1K tokens) compared to long-context baselines (over 22K tokens), making it suitable for resource-constrained deployments.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical and complementary roles of both Graph Memory for structural and temporal reasoning and Experience Memory for capturing reusable abstractions and cross-session generalization.
solution: A consolidation-centric tri-layer hybrid memory framework integrating Graph Memory (GM), Experience Memory (ExpM), and Passage Memory (PM) with explicit links between them.
Graph Memory (GM) organizes facts into a temporally grounded knowledge graph using LLM-based extraction, temporal normalization, and session-level verification to ensure consistency.

Experience Memory (ExpM) abstracts reusable patterns (preferences, strategies) through clustering, LLM-based induction, and online updates, while Passage Memory (PM) preserves original textual evidence.

A dual-channel memory retrieval strategy efficiently constructs compact reasoning contexts by jointly querying structured knowledge from the graph and supporting evidence from passages and experiences.
paper_summary.summary: MemWeaver introduces a tri-layer hybrid memory framework to enhance LLM agents' long-horizon reasoning by systematically consolidating interaction histories into structured facts, abstracted experiences, and raw passages.
